point_001: The work is seen as an incremental extension of prior research on temporal generalization in NLP tasks (sentiment analysis, machine translation, NER), with the main contribution being the application of similar ideas to generic language model pretraining rather than a fundamentally new approach.
point_002: The proposed mitigation strategy—dynamic/online evaluation—is a straightforward application of an existing method rather than a novel contribution, limiting the technical novelty of the paper.
point_003: Several conclusions drawn from the experiments are considered obvious or unsurprising (e.g., that performance degrades as the temporal gap widens, or that larger models do not solve the problem), reducing the perceived novelty of the findings.
point_004: The paper lacks an investigation into why dynamic evaluation is relatively ineffective, particularly for news data. A possible explanation—catastrophic forgetting—is not tested; a simple check would be to evaluate perplexity on non-temporally-shifted held-out data before and after dynamic evaluation.
point_005: In the closed-book QA experiments (Fig. 4), the paper attributes a performance drop to confusion around president names, but does not adequately address whether catastrophic forgetting during fine-tuning on 2011 QA data could explain the degradation in factual knowledge from the pre-training period.
point_006: The paper does not include a discussion of potential negative societal impacts of the work, such as how temporal adaptation of models might amplify biases, bake in historical prejudices, or otherwise cause harm—beyond a general 'limitations' section.
point_007: There are multiple figure/table reference errors throughout the manuscript (e.g., lines 149, 234, 245, 361, 368 incorrectly cite figures instead of tables), indicating insufficient proofreading.
